diff.description: The patient's primary cancer diagnosis details
diff.title: Primary cancer diagnosis

diff.description: The date (and optionally time) on which the medical conditions was first diagnosed.
diff.title: Diagnosis date
diff.rules:
Medical Oncology: 
1) Date of first (not necessarily definitive procedure) Histology OR Cytology specimen collected. 
2) Date Radiology used to make diagnosis was performed 
3) Date Tumour markers/bloods taken. 
4) Date of Clinical diagnosis. 
Haematology: 
1) Cytology/Histology (for example, bone marrow aspirate or peripheral blood in acute leukaemias, biopsy in lymphomas, biopsy needed in myeloma to confirm diagnosis). 
2) Flow cytometry (this is definitely diagnostic for conditions like CLL, and some other low grade B- and T- cell LPDs, sometimes confirms subtype of acute leukaemia). 
3) Cytogenetics (this includes FISH) and Molecular (for example, chronic phase CML, JAK+ MPN).

diff.description: The evidence on which the condition was diagnosed.
diff.title: Basis of diagnosis
Body structure part
diff.description: The anatomical part of the body structure being described.

diff.title: Landmark
diff.description: A significant landmark near the body site, e.g. nipple.

diff.title: Clockface position
diff.description: The clockface orientation from the landmark.

diff.title: Distance from landmark
diff.description: The distance from the given landmark.

diff.title: Comments
diff.description: Free text comments describing the body site.

diff.title: Morphology qualifier
diff.description: A histologic type that qualifies the main morphology, e.g. Hormone-producing tumour (for a NET), Molecularly defined gene alteration (for CNS)
The verification status to support or decline the clinical status of the condition or diagnosis (based on HL7 FHIR).
diff.title: Verification status
diff.sourceReference: https://hl7.org/fhir/valueset-condition-ver-status.html

The degree of differentiation of a tumour, e.g. the extent to which a tumour resembles the normal tissue at the site. The grade of the cancer is key to describing the diagnosis for which treatment is being prescribed, and for contextualising the treatment itself.
